text: According to IUPAC, the longest carbon chain is chosen as the parent chain. In 2-methylpentane, the parent chain has:
explanation: 2-methylpentane: parent chain = pentane (5 carbons), with methyl substituent at C-2. Full name: 2-methylpentane.
name: Parent Chain Selection

text: Inductive effect (–I) is the permanent displacement of σ-bond electrons due to electronegativity difference. Which group shows +I effect?
explanation: +I effect (electron-donating by induction): alkyl groups (–CH₃, –C₂H₅). –I effect (electron-withdrawing): –NO₂, –COOH, –Cl, –OH.
name: Inductive Effect

explanation: Resonance stabilisation is greatest when all contributing structures have equal (or similar) energies. Equal structures → more stabilisation.
name: Resonance Stabilisation

text: The hybridisation of carbon in benzene is:
explanation: Benzene: each C is sp² hybridised, forming 3 σ bonds. Unhybridised p orbitals overlap to form delocalised π system.
name: Hybridisation in Benzene

text: Markovnikov's rule states that in addition of HX to an unsymmetrical alkene:
options: H goes to the carbon with fewer H atoms
X goes to the carbon with fewer H atoms
H goes to the carbon with more H atoms
The reaction gives equal amounts of both products
explanation: Markovnikov's rule: H adds to the C with more hydrogens (H-rich carbon). This forms the more stable carbocation intermediate.
name: Markovnikov's Rule

text: Anti-Markovnikov addition (peroxide effect) occurs in addition of HBr to alkenes when:
explanation: In presence of peroxides, HBr adds via free radical mechanism (anti-Markovnikov). Br goes to carbon with more H (opposite to ionic). Only HBr shows this.
name: Peroxide Effect

text: The order of stability of carbocations is:
explanation: Carbocation stability: 3° > 2° > 1° > methyl. More alkyl groups → more +I effect → better stabilisation of positive charge.
name: Carbocation Stability

text: Electrophilic substitution (EAS) on benzene: the rate-determining step involves:
options: Loss of H⁺ from sigma complex
Addition of electrophile to all carbons
explanation: EAS mechanism: (1) Formation of π complex, (2) Formation of σ complex (arenium ion, slow, RDS), (3) Loss of H⁺ (fast) to restore aromaticity.
name: EAS Rate-Determining Step

text: For a compound to be aromatic by Hückel's rule, it must have:
options: (4n+2) π electrons in a planar cyclic conjugated system
An even number of π electrons
explanation: Hückel's rule: aromatic if planar, cyclic, fully conjugated, with (4n+2) π electrons (n = 0,1,2...). Benzene: n=1, 6π electrons.
name: Hückel's Rule

text: Which of the following is the correct order of acidic strength of hydrocarbons?
explanation: Acidic strength: alkyne (sp C, more electronegative) > alkene (sp²) > alkane (sp³). pKa: alkyne ≈ 25, alkene ≈ 44, alkane ≈ 50.
name: Acidity of Hydrocarbons

text: The IUPAC name of CH₃–CH=CH–CH₂–CH₃ is:
explanation: Double bond between C2 and C3. Parent chain: pentane. IUPAC: pent-2-ene (number from end nearest to double bond).
name: IUPAC Name of Alkene

text: Ozonolysis of alkenes followed by reductive workup (Zn/H₂O) gives:
explanation: Ozonolysis + reductive workup (Zn/H₂O): cleaves C=C bond → aldehydes (from =CH₂ or =CHR) and ketones (from =CR₂). Oxidative workup (H₂O₂) → carboxylic acids.
name: Ozonolysis Products

text: The reaction of alkanes with Cl₂ in UV light is:
explanation: Halogenation of alkanes: free radical substitution. Initiation: Cl₂ → 2Cl• (UV). Propagation: Cl• + CH₄ → CH₃• + HCl, then CH₃• + Cl₂ → CH₃Cl + Cl•.
name: Halogenation of Alkanes

text: In E2 elimination, the leaving group and the β-hydrogen must be:
explanation: E2 (bimolecular elimination): concerted, requires anti-periplanar arrangement (180° dihedral) of H and leaving group for effective overlap.
name: E2 Stereochemistry

explanation: Lindlar's catalyst (Pd/CaCO₃ + quinoline): partial hydrogenation of alkynes gives cis-alkenes (syn addition of H₂). Na/liquid NH₃ gives trans-alkenes.
name: Lindlar's Catalyst
